title: Public Transport
situation: Taking trains, trams, and buses in the Netherlands. Buying tickets and finding your way.

phrases.hindi: Naar Amsterdam Centraal, alstublieft. Een retour of enkele reis?
phrases.english: To Amsterdam Central, please. A return or single?
phrases.context: 'Retour' = return ticket, 'enkele reis' = single ticket. At the NS (Dutch rail) desk or machine. 'Alstublieft' is standard with requests. Amsterdam Centraal = 'AMS' in train apps.
phrases.pronunciation: naar am-ster-DAM sen-TRAAL, als-tu-BLEEFT. uhn re-TOUR of EN-ke-le RAYS?

phrases.hindi: Van welk spoor vertrekt de trein naar Utrecht?
phrases.english: From which platform does the train to Utrecht depart?
phrases.context: 'Spoor' = track/platform (Dutch trains use 'spoor', not 'perron' for the track number). 'Vertrekken' = to depart. You'll see 'spoor 4a' etc. on the boards. 'Wanneer vertrekt de trein?' = when does the train leave?
phrases.pronunciation: van WELK SPOOR ver-TREKT de TRAYN naar U-trecht?

phrases.hindi: Heeft u een OV-chipkaart? Ik wil een OV-chipkaart kopen.
phrases.english: Do you have an OV chip card? I want to buy an OV chip card.
phrases.context: The OV-chipkaart is the Dutch public transport card (like Oyster in London). Used for all trains, trams, buses, metros. You 'check in' (inchecken) and 'check out' (uitchecken) at yellow poles.
phrases.pronunciation: HAYFT u uhn OH-VAY-chip-kaart? ik WIL uhn OH-VAY-chip-kaart KOH-pen.

phrases.hindi: Is dit de juiste tram voor het Rijksmuseum?
phrases.english: Is this the right tram for the Rijksmuseum?
phrases.context: 'Juiste' = correct/right. 'Tram' lines are numbered in Dutch cities. Amsterdam has an extensive tram network. Always check by asking or using 9292 (the Dutch transit app).
phrases.pronunciation: is DIT de YOW-ste TRAM voor het RAYKS-mu-SAY-um?

phrases.hindi: Wat is de volgende halte? Moet ik hier overstappen?
phrases.english: What is the next stop? Do I need to transfer here?
phrases.context: 'Volgende halte' = next stop. 'Overstappen' = to transfer/change (trains/trams). Trains announce 'de volgende halte is...' You might also hear 'eindbestemming' = final destination.
phrases.pronunciation: wat is de VOL-gende HAL-te? MOOT ik HEER OH-ver-STAP-pen?

phrases.hindi: Waar kan ik een dagkaart kopen? Rijdt de bus ook op zondag?
phrases.english: Where can I buy a day pass? Does the bus run on Sundays too?
phrases.context: 'Dagkaart' = day pass. 'Rijden' = to drive/run (for vehicles). Buses/trains do run on Sundays in NL but less frequently. 'Dienstregeling' = timetable.
phrases.pronunciation: WAAR KAN ik uhn DACH-kaart KOH-pen? RAYT de BUS ook op ZON-dach?

phrases.hindi: De trein heeft vertraging. Hoeveel minuten vertraging?
phrases.english: The train is delayed. How many minutes delayed?
phrases.context: 'Vertraging' = delay — you will use this word a lot in NL. NS (Dutch rail) is famous for delays and cancellations ('uitgevallen'). Displays show 'vertraging X min'. Plan buffer time.
phrases.pronunciation: de TRAYN heeft ver-TRAA-ching. HOO-feel mi-NOO-ten ver-TRAA-ching?

phrases.hindi: Kunt u mij helpen? Ik zoek de uitgang / ingang / lift.
phrases.english: Can you help me? I'm looking for the exit / entrance / elevator.
phrases.context: 'Uitgang' = exit, 'ingang' = entrance, 'lift' = elevator. At large stations like Amsterdam Centraal, Rotterdam Centraal, Utrecht Centraal — always ask when lost. Staff wear NS uniforms (blue).
phrases.pronunciation: KUNT u may HEL-pen? ik ZOOK de OWT-chang / IN-chang / LIFT.

grammar_notes: 'Moeten + infinitive' for necessity: 'Moet ik hier uitstappen?' (Do I have to get off here?), 'Moet ik overstappen?' (Do I need to transfer?)
Separable verbs in transport: inchecken (in|checken), uitchecken (uit|checken), overstappen (over|stappen), uitstappen (uit|stappen), instappen (in|stappen)
'Van...naar' = from...to: 'Van Amsterdam naar Rotterdam' — very useful for buying tickets and asking directions
'Rijden' is used for vehicles: 'de bus rijdt', 'de trein rijdt'. 'Gaan' for people: 'ik ga naar Amsterdam'
culture_notes: The OV-chipkaart is essential for living in NL. Get one from train station service desks or GVB (Amsterdam transit) offices. Load money ('opladen') at machines
Always check in AND out — if you forget to check out, you get charged the maximum fare. Gates beep red if you haven't checked in
NS trains are punctual compared to many countries but Dutch people still complain. 'De trein had vertraging' is a very common excuse
Cycling is often faster in cities. 'OV-fiets' = rental bike from NS at train stations

skill_breakdown.explanation: At NS machines: choose 'enkele reis' (single) or 'retour' (return), select destination, choose class ('eerste klas' or 'tweede klas' — second is standard). You can also use the NS app or OV-chipkaart.

more_examples.hindi: Ik wil graag een treinkaartje naar Den Haag.
more_examples.english: I'd like a train ticket to The Hague.
Is there an early booking discount?
Can I take my bike along?

skill_breakdown.explanation: The OV-chipkaart is charged with money. You tap in ('inchecken') at yellow poles before boarding and tap out ('uitchecken') when you leave. Minimum balance needed: €0,20 bus/metro, €20 train (for checking in). Always check out!

more_examples.english: I want to top up my OV chip card.

more_examples.hindi: Hoeveel staat er op mijn kaart?
more_examples.english: How much is on my card?

practice_prompt: Navigating Dutch public transport — trains, trams, buses, buying tickets, and using the OV-chipkaart
